    "R1.1",

  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "What kind of check do you want on this abstract? Please choose one: (A) Writing polish only (clarity/grammar/concision), (B) Argument and evidence check (thesis framing, logical consistency, claim–result alignment, overclaim risks), or (C) Novelty/positioning focus (assessing the ‘first’ claim and contribution framing at a high level).",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "A conceptual pass is central to verify that the thesis and contribution framing are coherent for an abstract of a research system.",
    "R1.2": "The link from system design to reported outcomes needs checking for internal logical consistency.",
    "R1.3": "Quantitative results and causal attributions (e.g., checkpointing driving gains) require assessment for claim–evidence alignment.",
    "R1.6": "A generic ‘check’ frequently targets readability and clarity at the sentence/paragraph level in abstracts.",
    "RX.1": "Given the limited context, any assessment should explicitly note uncertainties imposed by having only the abstract.",
    "RX.2": "We should avoid reinforcing potentially overstated claims (e.g., ‘first’) without strong support.",
    "RX.3": "The review must stay within scope of the provided text and not infer beyond the abstract.",
    "RX.4": "Feedback should avoid encouraging misleading reporting practices or overconfident deployment claims.",
    "RX.5": "All observations should be traceable to the verbatim content provided, with inferences clearly labeled."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: What kind of check do you want on this abstract? Please choose one: (A) Writing polish only (clarity/grammar/concision), (B) Argument and evidence check (thesis framing, logical consistency, claim–result alignment, overclaim risks), or (C) Novelty/positioning focus (assessing the ‘first’ claim and contribution framing at a high level).

rationale.R1.1: A conceptual pass is central to verify that the thesis and contribution framing are coherent for an abstract of a research system.
rationale.R1.2: The link from system design to reported outcomes needs checking for internal logical consistency.
rationale.R1.3: Quantitative results and causal attributions (e.g., checkpointing driving gains) require assessment for claim–evidence alignment.
rationale.R1.6: A generic ‘check’ frequently targets readability and clarity at the sentence/paragraph level in abstracts.
rationale.RX.1: Given the limited context, any assessment should explicitly note uncertainties imposed by having only the abstract.
rationale.RX.2: We should avoid reinforcing potentially overstated claims (e.g., ‘first’) without strong support.
rationale.RX.3: The review must stay within scope of the provided text and not infer beyond the abstract.
rationale.RX.4: Feedback should avoid encouraging misleading reporting practices or overconfident deployment claims.
rationale.RX.5: All observations should be traceable to the verbatim content provided, with inferences clearly labeled.
